abstract.text: This article introduces the basic concepts of RFID to help you better understand the RFIDManager library.

inlineContent.text: The features in this library are built around RFID tags. Understanding the basic structure of an RFID tag makes the RFID-related features easier to follow.
The basic memory regions of an RFID tag are shown below:
An RFID tag contains four data regions: RESERVED, EPC, TID, and USER.
. Each field is 32 bits long, and the default value for both is
During inventory, the EPC data length depends on the upper 5 bits of the PC value. Examples:
For an EPC length of 6 words (96 bits): 6 words =
For an EPC length of 9 words (144 bits): 9 words =
For an EPC length of 15 words (240 bits): 15 words =
Contains only TID data. In general, TID is 96 bits long, unique, and cannot be modified.
Contains only USER data. Its length depends on the specific tag, and different tag types may provide different USER memory capacities.
represents the four RFID tag data regions, and
represents the tag data returned during inventory.
Inventory is used to quickly read data from a large number of RFID tags. In this library, you can start inventory by calling
. The inventoried tag data is returned through the
callback. You can also set the callback separately by using
, but these two approaches cannot be used at the same time.
During inventory, the module does not process other command requests except for a small number of commands, such as
. Do not send other commands to the module while inventory is running.
Tag filtering can be configured before inventory starts by calling
. These two methods have the same effect.
Filter tags based on the data content in the EPC, TID, or USER region.
For example, to filter tags whose EPC data starts with

code: "offset": 32,           // The first 32 bits are control data, so start from bit 32

inlineContent.text: For example, to filter RFID tags whose USER data from bit 16 to bit 48 is
If filtering is no longer needed and you want to clear it, configure
command reads data from a specified region of an RFID tag in a single operation.
The read-data command is different from the inventory command.
when you need to read a specific memory region precisely, such as the PC control word in the EPC region, which cannot be obtained through the inventory command alone.
when you need to quickly read data from a large number of tags.
command writes data to a specified region of an RFID tag.
If the target region has already been locked, the write operation requires the
command locks or unlocks a specified RFID tag region.
A locked tag region cannot be modified freely, which helps protect tag data from tampering.
password from the RESERVED region, and it cannot be the default value
command permanently disables an RFID tag. A killed tag can no longer be used.
Power is the signal strength transmitted by the reader antenna, measured in dBm. The supported range is
Higher power usually provides a longer reading distance, but it may also interfere with other devices or exceed local regulatory limits. Lower power is more suitable for precise, short-range reading.
Frequency is the radio band used by the reader during operation, measured in MHz.
Different regions have different regulatory limits, so the configured value should match local radio regulations.
RF Link refers to the communication parameter combination between the reader and the tag, which affects communication speed and stability.
For details about other parameters and their purpose, please consult the responsible team.

abstract.text: This tutorial will guide you through importing the RFIDManager library into your project and show you how to use it.
This article describes common issues that may arise when using RFIDManager.
